perturbation.original_text: For purposes of this Agreement, “Confidential Information” shall mean the fo llowing: i. All records, files, analysis, documents, software, computer or electronic data disks or tapes, test data, printouts, processes, designs, file layout, technical bulletins, manuals, diagrams, formulas, research, inventions, patents and discoveries reasonably related to the Parties’ businesses or products and services of the Parties that have not been publicly released; ii. Technical, financial, business plan and customer information, including standard periodic financial statements and analyses, budget s, tax returns, benefit and compensation plans, customer list(s) and contact names, functional and technical specifications; iii. Other valuable information disclosed by one of the Parties to another, designated as confidential expressly or by the circumstance s in which it is provided. Confidential Information does not include information that: i. Is lawfully in the public domain at the time it was transmitted; ii. Is independently known to or in possession of the receiving party at the time of receipt through no unlawful act; iii. Is disclosed by the receiving party with the prior written approval of the transmitting party; iv. Becomes known to the receiving party from a source other than the transmitting party, which third party legally is entitled to have and to disclose such information without restriction.
perturbation.changed_text: For purposes of this Agreement, “Confidential Information” shall mean information designated as confidential. Confidential Information does not include information that: i. Is lawfully in the public domain at the time it was transmitted; ii. Is independently known to or in possession of the receiving party at the time of receipt through no unlawful act; iii. Is disclosed by the receiving party with the prior written approval of the transmitting party; iv. Becomes known to the receiving party from a source other than the transmitting party, which third party legally is entitled to have and to disclose such information without restriction.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting the specific list of items considered 'Confidential Information,' and replacing it with a vague definition, the contract creates uncertainty. Section 4, which prohibits using Confidential Information for competitive advantage, becomes difficult to enforce because there's no clear, comprehensive list of what constitutes Confidential Information. The phrase 'information designated as confidential' creates ambiguity as it is not clear who designates it as such and when this designation has to occur.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Confidential Information disclosed to each of the Parties may include information that would provide the other party with an unfair competitive advantage if such Party were to use the Confidential Information for purposes other than those provided for hereunder. Accordingly, the Parties only agree to provide such Confidential Information to the other Party in return for this Party’s Agreement to be bound by each of the provisions in this Agreement including the provisions set forth in this section 4 and that such agreements by the Parties constitutes a material inducement for each Party to provide Confidential Information to the other Party and that such Confidentia l Information shall not be used by one of the Parties to obtain a competitive advantage against the other Party.
perturbation.justification: The original text provides a specific list of what constitutes 'Confidential Information'. The changed text replaces this with a vague definition: 'information designated as confidential.' This creates a contradiction because the 'Contradicted Text' relies on a clear definition of 'Confidential Information' to prevent unfair competitive advantage. The changed text's vagueness makes it unclear what information is protected, thus directly contradicting the purpose and enforceability of the contradicted section, making the agreement regarding competitive advantage unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: The R eceiving Party must protect the Confidential Information and, in doing so, must use no less than the highest degree of care that the Receiving Party applies to its own proprietary or secret information; The Confidential Information must be stored and hand led in such a way as to prevent unauthorized disclosure. The Receiving Party shall use its best efforts to limit dissemination of Disclosing Party’s “Confidential Information” to such of its employees or sub -contractor(s) who have a need to know for the aforesaid evaluation and/or development purposes.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party must protect the Confidential Information and, in doing so, must use a reasonable degree of care. The Receiving Party shall use its best efforts to limit dissemination of Disclosing Party’s “Confidential Information” to such of its employees or sub -contractor(s) who have a need to know for the aforesaid evaluation and/or development purposes.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the standard of care from 'highest degree' to 'reasonable degree' weakens the protection of Confidential Information. This affects Section 6 on Remedies, where demonstrating a breach of confidentiality is now more difficult. A 'reasonable' standard is subjective and open to interpretation, contradicting the intent of Section 6 to provide clear remedies for a breach. A reasonable degree of care is a lower legal standard, which may contradict the expected stringent protective measures implied elsewhere in the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: In the event one of the Parties or its officers, agents, employees or any of its subsidiaries or their officers, agent s or employees violates the restrictions against disclosure or use of the Confidential Information defined herein, Recipient or such entity or individual(s) as the case may be, shall and do hereby consent to the entry of an injunction by a court of compete nt jurisdiction against any further disclosure or use of the Confidential Information, and shall pay the The Recipient, including without limitation to any of its employees, subsidiaries , agents, subcontractors, officers shall indemnify and hold harmless against the Discloser against any unpermitted disclosing and publicizing. In event of any disclosing or publicizing the confidential information or breaching any obligations under this Agreement, the breaching party shall mitigate the losses and take for a ll reasonable measure, including the court injunction to prevent the disclosure and spread of confidential information upon the request of aggrieved party. The breaching party shall pay to non -beaching party a liquidated damage of 10,000 USD per occurrence . The total liquidated damage shall not exceed 1,0000,000 USD. The application of liquidated damage shall not prejudice the aggrieved party ’s right to claim for all the losses and shall not prejudice all the liabilities and obligations incurred . Nothing he re shall be interpreted as excluding consequential losses the aggrieved party may suffer, including without limitation to loss of profit, loss of goodwill, loss of business opportunities .
perturbation.justification: The original text stipulated the 'highest degree of care' for protecting Confidential Information. The changed text lowers this standard to a 'reasonable degree of care.' This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which outlines remedies, including injunctions and liquidated damages, for breaches of confidentiality. The original 'highest degree of care' standard provides a clear benchmark for assessing a breach. The lowered 'reasonable degree of care' introduces ambiguity, making it more difficult to prove a breach and enforce the remedies outlined in the contradicted section. This directly undermines the intent and enforceability of the remedies clause, creating a strong in-text contradiction as per the Omissions – In Text Contradiction definition, where essential language has been removed, creating a contradiction with obligations or constraints stated in other sections.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties shall not alter or remove from any Confidential Information any proprietary rights legend, copyright notice, trademark or trade secret legend, or any other mark id entifying the material as Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties shall make reasonable efforts not to alter or remove from any Confidential Information any proprietary rights legend, copyright notice, trademark or trade secret legend, or any other mark id entifying the material as Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall not' to 'shall make reasonable efforts' introduces ambiguity. While it maintains the necessity to keep confidential information marked, Section 6 about remedies for breaching confidentiality will be difficult to enforce since 'reasonable effort' is not definitive, which can lead to legal debates about what counts as breaking confidentiality. Adding 'reasonable efforts' creates a loophole that weakens the obligation and affects the enforceability of remedies.
perturbation.contradicted_text: In the event one of the Parties or its officers, agents, employees or any of its subsidiaries or their officers, agent s or employees violates the restrictions against disclosure or use of the Confidential Information defined herein, Recipient or such entity or individual(s) as the case may be, shall and do hereby consent to the entry of an injunction by a court of compete nt jurisdiction against any further disclosure or use of the Confidential Information, and shall pay the The Recipient, including without limitation to any of its employees, subsidiaries , agents, subcontractors, officers shall indemnify and hold harmless against the Discloser against any unpermitted disclosing and publicizing. In event of any disclosing or publicizing the confidential information or breaching any obligations under this Agreement, the breaching party shall mitigate the losses and take for a ll reasonable measure, including the court injunction to prevent the disclosure and spread of confidential information upon the request of aggrieved party. The breaching party shall pay to non -beaching party a liquidated damage of 10,000 USD per occurrence . The total liquidated damage shall not exceed 1,0000,000 USD. The application of liquidated damage shall not prejudice the aggrieved party ’s right to claim for all the losses and shall not prejudice all the liabilities and obligations incurred . Nothing he re shall be interpreted as excluding consequential losses the aggrieved party may suffer, including without limitation to loss of profit, loss of goodwill, loss of business opportunities .
perturbation.justification: The original text uses 'shall not,' imposing a strict prohibition. The changed text uses 'shall make reasonable efforts,' which introduces a subjective standard. The contradicted text outlines remedies for violations of confidentiality restrictions, including injunctions and liquidated damages. The changed text weakens the original obligation, making it more difficult to prove a violation justifying these remedies. Since it will be harder to prove that the 'reasonable efforts' were not made it breaks the consistency on how the contradicted text imposes strict penalties when breaching obligations.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties hereby agree that all materials, including financial reports and analyses, customer list(s) programs, software, documents, reports, statistical data, trade secrets or similar printed, coded, electronic or magnetic material made by one of the Parties or to come into one of the Parties’ possession in the course of negotiations shall be returned to the other Party, upon conclusion of the negotiations.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties hereby agree that all physical materials made by one of the Parties or to come into one of the Parties’ possession in the course of negotiations shall be returned to the other Party, upon conclusion of the negotiations.
perturbation.explanation: By limiting the returned materials to 'physical materials,' the clause contradicts the broader definition of 'Confidential Information' which includes electronic data. This creates an enforcement issue as Section 6 provides remedies for disclosure or use of Confidential Information. However, the electronic materials (which are part of the Confidential Information as per Section 1) do not need to be returned as they aren't physical, making it difficult to claim a breach.
perturbation.contradicted_text: In the event one of the Parties or its officers, agents, employees or any of its subsidiaries or their officers, agent s or employees violates the restrictions against disclosure or use of the Confidential Information defined herein, Recipient or such entity or individual(s) as the case may be, shall and do hereby consent to the entry of an injunction by a court of compete nt jurisdiction against any further disclosure or use of the Confidential Information, and shall pay the The Recipient, including without limitation to any of its employees, subsidiaries , agents, subcontractors, officers shall indemnify and hold harmless against the Discloser against any unpermitted disclosing and publicizing. In event of any disclosing or publicizing the confidential information or breaching any obligations under this Agreement, the breaching party shall mitigate the losses and take for a ll reasonable measure, including the court injunction to prevent the disclosure and spread of confidential information upon the request of aggrieved party. The breaching party shall pay to non -beaching party a liquidated damage of 10,000 USD per occurrence . The total liquidated damage shall not exceed 1,0000,000 USD. The application of liquidated damage shall not prejudice the aggrieved party ’s right to claim for all the losses and shall not prejudice all the liabilities and obligations incurred . Nothing he re shall be interpreted as excluding consequential losses the aggrieved party may suffer, including without limitation to loss of profit, loss of goodwill, loss of business opportunities .
perturbation.justification: The changed text narrows the scope of materials to be returned to 'physical materials.' The contradicted text discusses remedies for disclosure or use of 'Confidential Information,' which, according to the explanation, includes electronic data. By limiting the returned materials to physical items, the changed text creates a contradiction because it makes it difficult to enforce the confidential information clause, especially if the breach involves electronic data that the parties were not explicitly required to return. This aligns with the Omissions – In Text Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: The breaching party shall pay to non -beaching party a liquidated damage of 10,000 USD per occurrence . The total liquidated damage shall not exceed 1,0000,000 USD.
perturbation.changed_text: The breaching party may pay to non -beaching party a liquidated damage of 10,000 USD per occurrence . The total liquidated damage may not exceed 1,0000,000 USD.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall pay' to 'may pay' weakens the remedy for breach of confidentiality. Section 6 initially establishes a specific financial consequence for disclosing confidential information. However, changing shall to may make the payment of liquidated damages discretionary instead of mandatory and creates doubt about if there is even a financial consequence for the breach. Making the payment of damages discretionary directly affects the aggrieved party’s rights and ability to enforce the confidentiality obligations effectively, thus contradicting the intended purpose of the clause.
perturbation.contradicted_text: In the event one of the Parties or its officers, agents, employees or any of its subsidiaries or their officers, agent s or employees violates the restrictions against disclosure or use of the Confidential Information defined herein, Recipient or such entity or individual(s) as the case may be, shall and do hereby consent to the entry of an injunction by a court of compete nt jurisdiction against any further disclosure or use of the Confidential Information, and shall pay the The Recipient, including without limitation to any of its employees, subsidiaries , agents, subcontractors, officers shall indemnify and hold harmless against the Discloser against any unpermitted disclosing and publicizing. In event of any disclosing or publicizing the confidential information or breaching any obligations under this Agreement, the breaching party shall mitigate the losses and take for a ll reasonable measure, including the court injunction to prevent the disclosure and spread of confidential information upon the request of aggrieved party. The breaching party may pay to non -beaching party a liquidated damage of 10,000 USD per occurrence . The total liquidated damage may not exceed 1,0000,000 USD. The application of liquidated damage shall not prejudice the aggrieved party ’s right to claim for all the losses and shall not prejudice all the liabilities and obligations incurred . Nothing he re shall be interpreted as excluding consequential losses the aggrieved party may suffer, including without limitation to loss of profit, loss of goodwill, loss of business opportunities .
perturbation.justification: The original text states that the breaching party 'shall pay' liquidated damages, creating a mandatory obligation. The changed text replaces 'shall pay' with 'may pay', making the payment discretionary. The contradicted text states that the breaching party 'shall' mitigate losses and 'shall and do hereby consent' to an injunction, indicating mandatory actions and obligations. The change from 'shall' to 'may' in the changed text directly contradicts the mandatory language used in the contradicted text regarding remedies and obligations, creating a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement is governed by and shall be construed in accor dance with the laws of the People ’s Republic of China without regard to principles of conflict of law, and any dispute arising out of this Agreement or In connection with this Agreement shall be referred to Hong Kong International Arbitration Centre (HKIAC) for arbitration in accordance with Hong Kong Interna tional Arbitration Centre (HKIAC) administered arbitration rules in force at the time of application. The arbitrators shall be one (1). The place of arbitration shall be in Shen Zhen, China . The language used in arbitration proceeding shall be English Lang uage.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement is governed by and shall be construed in accor dance with the laws of the People’s Republic of China without regard to principles of conflict of law, and any dispute arising out of this Agreement or In connection with this Agreement may be referred to arbitration. The arbitrators may be one (1).
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be referred' to 'may be referred' makes arbitration optional. In Section 6, which refers to remedies for breach, there is a consent to the entry of an injunction by a court of competent jurisdiction, but the availability of arbitration becomes uncertain. This weakens the dispute resolution process, making the pursuit of remedies less predictable and more subject to court interpretations, contradicting Section 6.
perturbation.contradicted_text: In the event one of the Parties or its officers, agents, employees or any of its subsidiaries or their officers, agent s or employees violates the restrictions against disclosure or use of the Confidential Information defined herein, Recipient or such entity or individual(s) as the case may be, shall and do hereby consent to the entry of an injunction by a court of compete nt jurisdiction against any further disclosure or use of the Confidential Information, and shall pay the The Recipient, including without limitation to any of its employees, subsidiaries , agents, subcontractors, officers shall indemnify and hold harmless against the Discloser against any unpermitted disclosing and publicizing. In event of any disclosing or publicizing the confidential information or breaching any obligations under this Agreement, the breaching party shall mitigate the losses and take for a ll reasonable measure, including the court injunction to prevent the disclosure and spread of confidential information upon the request of aggrieved party. The breaching party may pay to non -beaching party a liquidated damage of 10,000 USD per occurrence . The total liquidated damage may not exceed 1,0000,000 USD. The application of liquidated damage shall not prejudice the aggrieved party ’s right to claim for all the losses and shall not prejudice all the liabilities and obligations incurred . Nothing he re shall be interpreted as excluding consequential losses the aggrieved party may suffer, including without limitation to loss of profit, loss of goodwill, loss of business opportunities .
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates arbitration for disputes, while the changed text makes it optional ('shall be referred' changed to 'may be referred'). This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which consents to injunctions by a court of competent jurisdiction as a remedy for breaches. By making arbitration optional, the enforceability and predictability of dispute resolution are weakened, potentially increasing reliance on court actions like injunctions, creating a strong in-text contradiction as the remedies pathway has been altered.
